_meta.authoringNotes: Etiology and management per AAO-HNS clinical practice guideline on improving voice outcomes after thyroid surgery and the AAO-HNS Vocal Cord Dysfunction Position Statement. Surgical evidence per Rosen et al. Laryngoscope 2014, Sulica Laryngoscope 2008 (PMID 18496091), and Paniello (medialization). Iatrogenic etiology per Jeannon et al. Int J Clin Pract 2009 (PMID 19222606). Bilateral abductor paralysis tracheostomy avoidance per Bosch et al.

h1Title: Vocal Cord Paralysis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Vocal cord paralysis is a loss of function of one or both vocal folds caused by injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve, the superior laryngeal nerve, or the vagus, presenting as breathy or weak voice, swallowing difficulty, and a higher risk of aspiration. Iatrogenic injury during thyroid, parathyroid, cervical spine, esophageal, cardiothoracic, or carotid surgery is the leading cause in adults, followed by malignant tumors (especially lung), idiopathic paralysis, intubation injury, viral neuropathy, and stroke. Unilateral paralysis affects roughly 1-2 per 1,000 people and produces a soft, breathy voice, vocal fatigue, throat clearing, and dysphagia for thin liquids. Bilateral paralysis is much rarer but can present with stridor, exercise intolerance, and life-threatening airway obstruction. Most unilateral cases improve over 6-12 months with voice therapy and temporary injection medialization; persistent dysphonia is treated with permanent medialization thyroplasty, arytenoid adduction, or reinnervation. Bilateral paralysis often requires airway intervention (tracheostomy, cordotomy, or arytenoidectomy) and is managed by laryngologists at high-volume centers.

keyStats.prevalence: Unilateral vocal cord paralysis affects approximately 1-2 per 1,000 individuals; bilateral is rare with no precise community prevalence but is overrepresented in thyroid surgical case series
keyStats.demographics: Iatrogenic causes predominate in adults (60-70% of cases); idiopathic or post-viral 15-25%; malignancy-related 10-20%. Female predominance reflects higher rates of thyroid surgery
keyStats.avgAge: Average age at diagnosis 50-65 in iatrogenic cases; congenital and birth-related paralysis presents in infancy
keyStats.globalCases: Procedure-related vocal cord paralysis estimated at 0.5-1.5% of thyroidectomies (transient) and 0.1-0.4% (permanent), with approximately 150,000 US thyroidectomies annually

definition: Vocal cord paralysis (ICD-10: J38.00 unspecified, J38.01 unilateral, J38.02 bilateral) is impairment of vocal fold motion caused by injury or dysfunction of the motor nerves controlling laryngeal muscles. The recurrent laryngeal nerve, a branch of the vagus (cranial nerve X), innervates all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid; injury produces ipsilateral immobility of the affected vocal fold. The superior laryngeal nerve, also a branch of the vagus, innervates the cricothyroid and is responsible for vocal pitch control; its injury causes a weak, breathy voice and difficulty with high notes. Vagus injury proximal to both branches produces combined sensory and motor deficits including loss of sensation in the supraglottis (aspiration risk). The clinical syndrome ranges from mild dysphonia to airway compromise, depending on whether one or both folds are affected and on the final position of the paralyzed fold (paramedian, intermediate, or lateral). The 2021 European Laryngological Society position paper and AAO-HNS practice guidelines classify cases by laterality, position of the paralyzed fold, time course (recent <12 months, persistent ≥12 months), and underlying etiology. Management is guided by laryngology and includes thorough nerve-tracing evaluation, voice therapy, temporary or permanent surgical medialization, reinnervation, and airway procedures for bilateral disease. Laryngologists, head and neck surgeons, neurologists, speech-language pathologists, and pulmonologists collaborate in care, with referral to high-volume centers recommended for complex bilateral cases and revision surgery.

typesClassification.description: Loss of motion of one vocal fold, most often from recurrent laryngeal nerve injury during thyroid, cervical spine, esophageal, mediastinal, or carotid surgery. Presents with breathy hoarse voice, vocal fatigue, choking on liquids, and weak cough. Left side is affected more often than right because of the longer course of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve through the chest.

Loss of motion of both vocal folds. Often presents with airway obstruction, stridor on exertion or at rest, and relatively preserved voice (because the folds remain close together). Major causes are bilateral thyroid surgery, brainstem stroke, neurodegenerative disease, and idiopathic. Often requires airway intervention.

Injury to the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve, often during thyroid or carotid surgery. Causes a weak, breathy voice with loss of high pitch and is often missed by routine flexible laryngoscopy because the affected fold still moves. Singers and professional voice users are particularly affected.

typesClassification.type: Combined vagus or proximal cranial-nerve-X injury
typesClassification.description: Injury proximal to the branching of the recurrent and superior laryngeal nerves. Produces both laryngeal motor deficit and loss of supraglottic sensation (high aspiration risk) and often pharyngeal weakness. Causes include skull-base tumors, cerebellopontine angle lesions, and high vagal nerve sheath tumors.

typesClassification.type: Paradoxical vocal fold motion (vocal cord dysfunction)
typesClassification.description: Functional, episodic, non-paralytic adduction of the vocal folds during inspiration that mimics asthma and stridor. Distinct from true paralysis; managed by speech-language pathology and behavioral therapy, often without surgery. Listed for differential diagnosis.

primarySymptoms: Breathy, soft, hoarse voice that lacks projection — the hallmark of unilateral paralysis caused by air escaping through the gap between the immobile fold and the moving fold.
Vocal fatigue with prolonged speaking, throat tightness, and effortful phonation; voice often improves with rest and worsens by the end of the day.
Choking, coughing, or throat clearing during meals and especially with thin liquids (water, broth), indicating glottic insufficiency and aspiration risk.
Weak, ineffective cough and reduced ability to clear secretions, raising the risk of aspiration pneumonia.
Shortness of breath on exertion and reduced exercise tolerance from inefficient phonation and increased respiratory effort.
Sensation of throat dryness, lump in throat (globus), or constant urge to clear the throat.
Stridor (noisy breathing, especially inspiratory) at rest or on exertion in bilateral paralysis — may require urgent airway evaluation.
Loss of high vocal pitch and reduced volume in superior laryngeal nerve injury, particularly noticed by singers and professional voice users.
Speech that tires the listener, requires the speaker to repeat themselves, and limits social or occupational interactions.
In children: weak cry, feeding difficulty, recurrent pneumonia, or noisy breathing.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent hoarseness lasting more than 2-3 weeks, especially after recent surgery (thyroid, cervical spine, cardiothoracic) or intubation
Coughing or choking during drinking, particularly with thin liquids
Change in singing voice, loss of upper range, or breathiness in a professional voice user
Stridor or unexplained shortness of breath on mild exertion
Recurrent pneumonia or unexplained chronic cough in a patient at risk for aspiration
emergencySigns: Stridor at rest or rapidly progressive stridor on exertion — airway compromise; assess in the emergency department
Significant aspiration with cyanosis, oxygen desaturation, or new respiratory distress
Severe hoarseness with associated dysphagia and weight loss — exclude malignancy with urgent imaging and endoscopy
Acute onset of dysphonia with new neurological deficit (slurred speech, facial droop, limb weakness) — possible brainstem stroke
Rapidly progressive bilateral vocal cord paralysis after recent thyroidectomy — surgical emergency for airway management

causes.description: The leading cause of unilateral and bilateral vocal cord paralysis in adults. Thyroid and parathyroid surgery accounts for 30-50% of iatrogenic cases; cervical spine surgery (anterior cervical discectomy and fusion), esophageal surgery, cardiothoracic procedures (especially patent ductus, aortic arch), and carotid endarterectomy are other common sources. Transient paralysis rates after thyroidectomy are 0.5-1.5% per nerve at risk; permanent rates 0.1-0.4%.

Lung cancer (left recurrent laryngeal nerve traversing the aortopulmonary window is particularly vulnerable), thyroid cancer, esophageal cancer, mediastinal lymphoma, and skull-base tumors can compress or invade the vagus or recurrent laryngeal nerve. New-onset unilateral vocal cord paralysis without a clear surgical cause requires CT or MRI from skull base to mid-chest.

Approximately 15-25% of cases have no identifiable cause; many are attributed to post-viral neuropathy (often after URI), and a subset may recover spontaneously over 6-12 months. Diagnosis is made after thorough imaging and laboratory exclusion of other causes.

Prolonged or traumatic endotracheal intubation, especially with large tubes, can injure the arytenoid joint, cricoarytenoid musculature, or recurrent laryngeal nerve. Includes prolonged ICU intubation and emergency procedures with difficult airway.

Brainstem stroke (Wallenberg syndrome involves nucleus ambiguus), multiple sclerosis, Parkinson's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, multiple system atrophy, and Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease can cause unilateral or bilateral paralysis. ALS frequently presents with bulbar findings including vocal fold weakness.

Lyme disease, herpes zoster (Ramsay Hunt syndrome), Epstein-Barr virus, and granulomatous diseases (sarcoidosis, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, tuberculosis, syphilis) can produce vocal cord paralysis. Auto-immune diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis are less common causes.

Birth trauma, Arnold-Chiari malformation, hydrocephalus, and congenital cardiac anomalies can produce neonatal vocal cord paralysis. Often presents with stridor, weak cry, feeding difficulty, or apnea. Many resolve spontaneously over 6-12 months.

riskFactors.factor: Recent neck, chest, or skull-base surgery
riskFactors.description: Thyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy, anterior cervical spine surgery, esophagectomy, mediastinal tumor resection, cardiac surgery, and carotid endarterectomy all place the recurrent laryngeal or vagus nerves at risk. Intraoperative nerve monitoring reduces but does not eliminate injury.

riskFactors.factor: Lung, thyroid, esophageal, or mediastinal malignancy
riskFactors.description: Tumor compression or invasion is responsible for 10-20% of cases; left-sided unilateral paralysis without surgical cause warrants imaging from skull base through mid-chest.
ICU intubation longer than 7-10 days, large tube size, and traumatic intubation increase the risk of cricoarytenoid joint injury and recurrent laryngeal nerve neuropraxia.

riskFactors.factor: Brainstem stroke or neurodegenerative disease (ALS, MS, Parkinson's, MSA)
riskFactors.description: Affect bulbar nuclei or peripheral nerves; bilateral paralysis is more common in neurodegenerative disease and may progress.
Idiopathic post-viral neuropathy accounts for some cases; many recover spontaneously over 6-12 months. Specific viruses (HSV, VZV, EBV, influenza) have been implicated.

Lyme disease, Ramsay Hunt syndrome (VZV), sarcoidosis, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, syphilis, and tuberculosis are uncommon but treatable causes that should be considered in atypical or progressive cases.

affectedDemographics: Iatrogenic causes (60-70% of adult cases) reflect the demographics of the procedures involved: middle-aged adults undergoing thyroid surgery (female predominance) and older adults undergoing cardiothoracic and cervical spine surgery.
Malignancy-related paralysis is more common in adults over 60 and in smokers.
Pediatric vocal cord paralysis is most often congenital, post-cardiac surgical (patent ductus or aortic arch repair), or related to Arnold-Chiari malformation.
Approximately 1-2% of patients undergoing thyroidectomy experience transient paralysis; 0.1-0.4% develop permanent paralysis (AAO-HNS practice guideline).
Professional voice users (singers, teachers, sales professionals, lawyers, clergy) experience greater functional impact and benefit from rapid intervention.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines history, perceptual voice evaluation, flexible laryngoscopy, and targeted imaging or laboratory testing to identify the underlying cause. The history establishes the time course of symptoms, recent surgery, intubation, or trauma; smoking and cancer risk factors; respiratory and swallowing symptoms; and prior voice complaints. Perceptual voice evaluation by an experienced clinician or speech-language pathologist documents breathiness, roughness, strain, and reduced loudness using the GRBAS or CAPE-V scale. Flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy is the cornerstone test, performed in the office in 5-10 minutes through the nose, and directly visualizes vocal fold motion during quiet breathing, sniff, phonation, and pitch glides. Stroboscopy adds slow-motion assessment of vibratory function and confirms findings. The position of the paralyzed fold (paramedian, intermediate, paramedian-lateral) and the gap with the contralateral fold guides treatment selection. Laryngeal electromyography (LEMG) is the only test that directly examines the nerve and helps distinguish neuropraxia (likely to recover) from denervation (unlikely to recover); it is performed 1-6 months after injury and guides decisions about temporary vs. permanent surgery. When no surgical or obvious cause is identified, contrast-enhanced CT or MRI from the skull base through the upper mediastinum is performed to look for malignancy, brainstem lesion, or compressive mass. Additional workup may include thyroid imaging and function tests, chest X-ray, swallow evaluation (modified barium swallow or FEES), and laboratory workup for Lyme disease, syphilis, sarcoidosis (ACE), autoimmune disease (ANA, ANCA), and vitamin deficiencies. Multidisciplinary evaluation with laryngology, speech pathology, and as needed neurology, oncology, and pulmonology yields the highest diagnostic accuracy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of vocal fold motion at rest, during breathing, sniffing, phonation, and pitch glides; identifies paralysis, paresis, and structural lesions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Office procedure of 5-10 minutes using a thin flexible scope passed through the nose after topical anesthesia and decongestant spray. Mild discomfort; results immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Slow-motion examination of vocal fold vibration to characterize closure pattern, mucosal wave, and gap; refines diagnosis and quantifies severity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Office procedure of 15-20 minutes using a rigid or flexible scope with stroboscopic light. Video recording shared with the patient and team.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct nerve and muscle electrophysiologic testing; distinguishes neuropraxia, axonotmesis, and chronic denervation; predicts likelihood of recovery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Office procedure of 30-45 minutes with small needle electrodes placed in laryngeal muscles after topical anesthesia. Generally well tolerated; results within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Contrast-enhanced CT skull base to mid-chest
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies malignancy, mass, or structural lesion compressing the vagus or recurrent laryngeal nerve
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5-10 minute CT with intravenous contrast through a small cannula. Standard imaging when no surgical or obvious cause is identified; results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes brainstem stroke, demyelinating disease, skull-base tumor, or proximal vagal lesion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the scanner. Used selectively when CT is non-diagnostic or central neurological signs are present.

diagnosticTests.test: Swallowing evaluation (modified barium swallow or FEES)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies aspiration risk and guides dietary recommendations
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Modified barium swallow takes 15-20 minutes in radiology with various consistencies. FEES (fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing) is done in the office; both performed by speech-language pathology and ENT.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes infectious, autoimmune, and metabolic causes when no clear etiology is identified
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. May include Lyme serology, syphilis testing, ACE level, ANA, ANCA, vitamin B12, and TSH, depending on clinical suspicion.

treatmentOverview: Management is individualized by laterality, etiology, expected recovery, vocal demands, and aspiration risk. For unilateral paralysis, the first step is treatment of the underlying cause (resection of compressive tumor, treatment of infection) when identified. Voice therapy with a laryngology-trained speech-language pathologist begins early and includes glottal closure exercises, vocal function exercises, and compensatory strategies. For patients with significant dysphonia or aspiration not responding to voice therapy alone, temporary injection medialization with absorbable material (carboxymethylcellulose, hyaluronic acid) is performed in the office or operating room within weeks to months of onset. Injection results in immediate voice improvement, gives the patient time for spontaneous recovery, and serves as a diagnostic trial before permanent surgery. Permanent surgical treatments for persistent paralysis (typically after 9-12 months of observation) include medialization laryngoplasty (type 1 thyroplasty with Silastic or Gore-Tex implant), arytenoid adduction (adds rotational correction for vertical level mismatch), and laryngeal reinnervation (ansa cervicalis to recurrent laryngeal nerve anastomosis), with reinnervation increasingly favored in younger patients with reliable nerve anatomy. Outcomes are excellent: medialization improves voice in 85-95% of patients; reinnervation produces stable, dynamic voice in 70-80%. For bilateral paralysis, airway management is the priority. Acute presentation may require emergency intubation or tracheostomy. Persistent bilateral paralysis options include long-term tracheostomy (most reliable airway), endoscopic posterior cordotomy or partial arytenoidectomy (improve airway at the cost of voice), and selective reinnervation (investigational). Bilateral injection of botulinum toxin into the thyroarytenoid is an alternative in adductor spasmodic patterns. Aspiration risk is managed with diet modification (thickened liquids), postural maneuvers, and in selected cases laryngotracheal separation procedures or feeding tube placement. Long-term follow-up combines laryngology, speech pathology, and management of underlying disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Voice therapy with a laryngology-trained SLP
medicalTreatments.description: Structured 6-12 session program of glottal closure exercises, vocal function exercises, semi-occluded vocal tract exercises, breathing coordination, and compensatory strategies. Begins as soon as paralysis is recognized. Continues alongside or after any surgical intervention.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves voice quality and reduces effort in 60-80% of patients with unilateral paralysis; particularly effective when started early; non-invasive first-line treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Office-based injection medialization (temporary)
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of absorbable material (carboxymethylcellulose, hyaluronic acid, calcium hydroxylapatite) into the paralyzed fold to medialize it. Performed in the office under topical anesthesia or in the operating room. Lasts 2-6 months depending on material.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate voice improvement in 80-90%; reduces aspiration risk; serves as diagnostic trial before permanent surgery; effects last 2-6 months allowing time for spontaneous recovery

medicalTreatments.name: Permanent medialization laryngoplasty (type 1 thyroplasty)
medicalTreatments.description: Open surgical implantation of Silastic, Gore-Tex, or titanium implant to permanently medialize the paralyzed fold. Performed under local anesthesia with sedation so the patient can vocalize during the procedure to optimize position.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves voice in 85-95% of patients; durable for years to decades; performed after 9-12 months of observation or earlier if recovery clearly unlikely

medicalTreatments.name: Arytenoid adduction
medicalTreatments.description: Adds rotational correction by tilting the arytenoid cartilage to close vertical-level mismatch; performed alone or combined with medialization laryngoplasty in cases with significant posterior gap.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves voice in 80-90% when combined with medialization; particularly useful when posterior glottic gap persists after medialization alone

medicalTreatments.name: Laryngeal reinnervation (ansa cervicalis to recurrent laryngeal nerve)
medicalTreatments.description: Microsurgical anastomosis of the ansa cervicalis or another donor nerve to the distal recurrent laryngeal nerve. Produces tonic reinnervation without restoring volitional motion. Increasingly favored in younger patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stable, dynamic voice in 70-80% at 6-12 months; benefit may take 4-9 months to develop; long-term durability comparable to medialization in modern series

medicalTreatments.name: Endoscopic posterior cordotomy or partial arytenoidectomy (bilateral paralysis)
medicalTreatments.description: Endoscopic laser removal of part of the vocal fold or arytenoid to enlarge the airway. Performed for bilateral paralysis to allow decannulation of tracheostomy. Voice may be permanently weakened.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Decannulation rate 70-85% after one to two procedures; voice quality reduced compared with pre-operative baseline

surgicalOptions.name: Tracheostomy (bilateral paralysis with airway compromise)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical airway placed below the larynx to bypass vocal fold obstruction. Used emergently or electively in bilateral paralysis with stridor at rest or exertional limitation. Can be permanent or a bridge to definitive airway-enlarging procedures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 100% airway protection; long-term tracheostomy management with humidification, suctioning, and tube changes; lifestyle impact significant

surgicalOptions.name: Selective laryngeal reinnervation (bilateral paralysis, investigational)
surgicalOptions.description: Microsurgical reinnervation of abductor muscles to restore inspiratory vocal fold opening. Limited to specialized centers and primarily investigational. Aims to restore airway without tracheostomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional airway in approximately 70% in early case series; long-term outcomes still being characterized

surgicalOptions.name: Laryngotracheal separation (severe aspiration)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical separation of the airway from the digestive tract for patients with severe chronic aspiration and life-threatening pneumonia. Voice is sacrificed in exchange for airway protection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolves aspiration pneumonia in 90%+; reserved for patients with severe neurological disease and no realistic voice rehabilitation prospect

alternativeTreatments.name: Lee Silverman Voice Treatment (LSVT LOUD) for neurological cases
alternativeTreatments.description: Intensive voice therapy program developed for Parkinson's disease and adapted for other neurological voice disorders. Four sessions per week for four weeks with focus on increasing vocal loudness.

alternativeTreatments.name: Postural and dietary modifications for aspiration management
alternativeTreatments.description: Chin-tuck swallow maneuver, head turn to the paralyzed side, thickened liquids, and small-volume bolus reduce aspiration risk while waiting for recovery or surgical intervention.

whySeeSpecialist: Laryngologists (subspecialty-trained ENT surgeons) provide the highest level of diagnostic and surgical expertise for vocal cord paralysis. They perform stroboscopy, laryngeal EMG, office-based injections, and complex phonosurgical procedures. Speech-language pathologists trained in voice therapy are essential partners. For bilateral paralysis, malignancy-related cases, and revision surgery, referral to a high-volume academic laryngology program is recommended.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a laryngologist (ENT with fellowship training in laryngology) at a high-volume voice center. Confirm in-office stroboscopy, injection capability, and access to voice therapy services. For bilateral paralysis, prefer a center performing reinnervation, posterior cordotomy, and complex airway reconstruction.
hospitalCriteria: Subspecialty laryngology service with stroboscopy and laryngeal EMG
Phonosurgery operating room with regional anesthesia capability
Multidisciplinary head and neck oncology service for malignancy-related cases
keyFacilities: Comprehensive head and neck oncology programs
Speech-language pathology services with voice specialty
Pediatric otolaryngology programs for congenital cases
preventionStrategies: Choose high-volume surgeons and centers for thyroid, parathyroid, cervical spine, esophageal, and cardiothoracic procedures; outcomes correlate strongly with surgeon volume.
Insist on intraoperative recurrent laryngeal nerve monitoring during thyroid and parathyroid surgery — reduces but does not eliminate injury rates.
Address smoking and lung cancer screening risk factors; lung cancer is a leading non-iatrogenic cause of vocal cord paralysis.
Treat reflux and chronic vocal abuse early to prevent secondary vocal pathology in patients with subtle paralysis.
Seek prompt ENT evaluation for any hoarseness lasting more than 2-3 weeks, especially in patients with risk factors for malignancy.
Confirm vaccination against varicella zoster in adults aged 50 and older to reduce Ramsay Hunt syndrome risk.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking — reduces risk of underlying malignancy and improves recovery from any surgical intervention.
Use vocal hygiene practices: regular hydration, voice rest between effortful speaking, and avoidance of yelling and throat clearing.
Adjust dietary consistency (thickened liquids, smaller bites, chin-tuck swallow) for known aspiration risk while undergoing treatment.
Adhere to scheduled voice therapy sessions and home practice exercises.
Use voice amplification devices (headset microphone) in occupational settings to reduce vocal strain.
Manage reflux with dietary measures and proton-pump inhibitors when indicated to optimize laryngeal recovery.

recommended: Adequate hydration (≥2 L/day) to maintain mucosal moisture and vocal fold lubrication
Thickened liquids (nectar or honey consistency) for patients with documented aspiration on swallow study
Soft moist foods that require less effort to swallow during the acute recovery phase
Anti-reflux dietary pattern: smaller frequent meals, avoid eating within 3 hours of bedtime
avoid: Alcohol and caffeine, which dry the vocal folds and worsen reflux
Acidic and spicy foods that exacerbate laryngopharyngeal reflux
Very hot foods and beverages during the immediate post-operative period
Carbonated beverages that increase reflux symptoms in susceptible patients

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is encouraged for general health and as adjunct to voice therapy. Avoid breath-holding (Valsalva) maneuvers and heavy lifting in the first 6 weeks after phonosurgical procedures. Vocal warm-ups before extended speaking and after surgery are recommended, supervised by a speech-language pathologist.
dailyManagement: Practice prescribed voice therapy exercises daily, including resonant voice, semi-occluded vocal tract, and breath support exercises.
Hydrate with at least 2 liters of water daily; avoid caffeine and alcohol that dry the vocal folds.
Modify diet consistency if aspiration risk is present; use chin-tuck or head turn as advised by the SLP.
Use voice amplification (microphone) for occupational speaking demands.
Track voice quality, swallowing safety, and any new respiratory symptoms; report stridor or new shortness of breath promptly.
Attend follow-up laryngoscopy at 3, 6, and 12 months to monitor recovery; LEMG may be repeated to track reinnervation.
prognosis: Outcomes depend on cause and time course. Approximately 30-40% of idiopathic and post-viral cases recover spontaneously within 6-12 months; iatrogenic transient paralysis recovers in 50-70% within 6 months; permanent paralysis after thyroid surgery is 0.1-0.4%. Voice therapy and injection medialization restore acceptable voice in 80-90% of patients with persistent unilateral paralysis. Permanent surgical treatment (medialization laryngoplasty, arytenoid adduction, reinnervation) produces excellent voice outcomes in 80-95% of patients. Aspiration risk is reduced substantially by surgical medialization. For bilateral paralysis, airway management is the priority; quality of life depends on whether decannulation is achieved and at what cost to voice. Malignancy-related paralysis carries the prognosis of the underlying disease. Long-term outcomes are best with multidisciplinary care, treatment at high-volume centers, and adherence to voice therapy alongside surgical interventions.
recoveryTimeline: Spontaneous recovery, if it occurs, typically within 6-12 months of onset. Office injection medialization produces immediate voice change lasting 2-6 months. Voice therapy yields measurable improvement over 6-12 sessions. Permanent medialization laryngoplasty: voice improvement immediate, with optimization over 4-6 weeks. Reinnervation: benefit develops over 4-9 months as nerve regenerates. Bilateral paralysis surgical decannulation usually achieved after 1-2 endoscopic procedures.
complications: Aspiration pneumonia from glottic insufficiency and weak cough — manage with diet modification and surgical medialization.
Airway obstruction in bilateral paralysis — may require emergency tracheostomy.
Persistent dysphonia limiting occupational and social function, particularly in professional voice users.
Vocal fatigue, vocal abuse, and secondary vocal-fold pathology from compensatory hyperfunction.
Surgical complications of phonosurgery: infection, implant extrusion, airway compromise during revision, and incomplete voice restoration.
Psychological impact: depression, social withdrawal, and reduced quality of life from chronic dysphonia and feeding limitations.

supportResources.name: National Spasmodic Dysphonia Association (also covers vocal fold paralysis)
supportResources.description: Patient education, support groups, and clinician directory for voice disorders including vocal cord paralysis.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery
supportResources.description: Professional society with patient resources and 'Find an ENT' directory; AAO-HNS practice guidelines on hoarseness and post-thyroid voice.

supportResources.name: The Voice Foundation
supportResources.description: Professional and patient organization promoting voice health and research.

supportResources.name: American Speech-Language-Hearing Association (ASHA)
supportResources.description: Directory of speech-language pathologists and patient information on voice and swallowing disorders.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dysphonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dysphonia is a general term for any voice abnormality (hoarseness, breathiness, strain). Vocal cord paralysis is one specific cause. Many cases of dysphonia have other causes (laryngitis, vocal nodules, polyps, muscle tension dysphonia, reflux) requiring different management. Diagnosis requires laryngoscopy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma produces expiratory wheezing, reversible airway obstruction, and responds to bronchodilators. Bilateral vocal cord paralysis and paradoxical vocal fold motion produce inspiratory stridor that does not respond to bronchodilators. Laryngoscopy during symptoms distinguishes them; misdiagnosis is common.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Brainstem stroke (especially lateral medullary or Wallenberg syndrome) can cause vocal cord paralysis along with other neurological deficits. Vocal cord paralysis without other neurological signs is rarely stroke. New-onset vocal cord paralysis with focal neurological findings warrants urgent imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Dysphonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Broader category of voice disorders; vocal cord paralysis is one specific cause requiring laryngoscopy for diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Brainstem stroke is a recognized cause of acute vocal cord paralysis with associated neurological deficits.

relatedConditions.name: Parkinson's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Neurodegenerative cause of laryngeal muscle weakness; managed with LSVT voice therapy and supportive care.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Demyelinating disease that can affect bulbar nerves and cause vocal cord paralysis as part of broader neurological picture.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently confused with bilateral vocal cord paralysis or paradoxical vocal fold motion; laryngoscopy distinguishes them.

faqs.question: What is vocal cord paralysis?
faqs.answer: Vocal cord paralysis is loss of motion of one or both vocal folds caused by injury or dysfunction of the recurrent laryngeal nerve, superior laryngeal nerve, or vagus. It produces breathy hoarse voice, swallowing difficulty, aspiration risk, and in bilateral cases airway obstruction with stridor.

faqs.question: What causes vocal cord paralysis?
faqs.answer: Common causes include surgery (thyroid, parathyroid, cervical spine, cardiothoracic, esophageal, carotid), malignancy (especially lung cancer compressing the left recurrent laryngeal nerve), idiopathic post-viral neuropathy, prolonged intubation, brainstem stroke, neurodegenerative disease, and rare infectious or autoimmune causes.

faqs.question: How is vocal cord paralysis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy in the office, which directly visualizes vocal fold motion. Laryngeal videostroboscopy refines the picture, and laryngeal EMG distinguishes neuropraxia from chronic denervation. Imaging from skull base through mid-chest is added when no surgical or obvious cause is identified.

faqs.question: Can vocal cord paralysis get better on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes, in some cases. About 30-40% of idiopathic and post-viral cases recover spontaneously within 6-12 months. Iatrogenic transient paralysis after thyroidectomy recovers in 50-70% within 6 months. Cases persisting beyond 9-12 months are unlikely to recover and benefit from permanent surgical intervention.

faqs.question: How is vocal cord paralysis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment starts with voice therapy and, when needed, office injection medialization to immediately improve voice and reduce aspiration risk. Persistent paralysis is treated permanently with medialization laryngoplasty, arytenoid adduction, or laryngeal reinnervation. Bilateral paralysis often requires airway intervention (tracheostomy, cordotomy).

faqs.question: Is vocal cord paralysis the same as vocal cord dysfunction?
faqs.answer: No. Vocal cord paralysis is loss of motion due to nerve injury; vocal cord dysfunction (paradoxical vocal fold motion) is intermittent functional adduction during inspiration mimicking asthma. They are managed differently — paralysis often requires surgery, dysfunction responds to speech therapy and behavioral interventions.

faqs.question: Will I aspirate food and liquids with vocal cord paralysis?
faqs.answer: Some patients aspirate, especially with thin liquids, due to the glottic gap. Swallow evaluation determines risk. Treatments include dietary modification (thickened liquids), swallow maneuvers (chin tuck), and surgical medialization which significantly reduces aspiration risk.

faqs.question: What is injection medialization?
faqs.answer: Injection medialization is an office-based procedure where absorbable material (hyaluronic acid, carboxymethylcellulose, or calcium hydroxylapatite) is injected into the paralyzed vocal fold to push it toward the midline. It produces immediate voice improvement lasting 2-6 months and serves as a trial before permanent surgery.

faqs.question: What is thyroplasty?
faqs.answer: Thyroplasty (medialization laryngoplasty, type 1) is a permanent surgical procedure that places a Silastic, Gore-Tex, or titanium implant through a small window in the thyroid cartilage to push the paralyzed fold medially. It is done under local anesthesia with sedation so the patient can vocalize during placement. Voice improves in 85-95%.

faqs.question: What is laryngeal reinnervation?
faqs.answer: Laryngeal reinnervation is a microsurgical procedure that connects a donor nerve (typically the ansa cervicalis) to the distal recurrent laryngeal nerve to restore tonic muscle activity in the paralyzed fold. Benefit develops over 4-9 months. Increasingly favored in younger patients for durable voice outcomes.

faqs.question: Why is the left vocal cord more often affected?
faqs.answer: The left recurrent laryngeal nerve takes a longer course, descending into the chest, looping around the aortic arch, and ascending back to the larynx. This longer trajectory exposes it to mediastinal pathology including lung cancer, aortic aneurysm, and cardiothoracic surgical injury. The right nerve has a shorter course around the subclavian artery.

faqs.question: Does vocal cord paralysis after thyroid surgery always recover?
faqs.answer: No. Transient paralysis (≤6 months) occurs in about 0.5-1.5% of nerves at risk and most recover; permanent paralysis after thyroidectomy occurs in approximately 0.1-0.4%. Risk is higher with malignancy, revision surgery, large goiter, and central neck dissection. Intraoperative nerve monitoring reduces but does not eliminate risk.

faqs.question: Can I sing again after vocal cord paralysis?
faqs.answer: Many singers recover usable singing voice after voice therapy combined with injection or permanent surgical medialization, though high-end performance ability may not fully return. Outcomes are best with prompt diagnosis, treatment at a high-volume voice center, and intensive voice therapy alongside surgery.

faqs.question: Is vocal cord paralysis painful?
faqs.answer: Vocal cord paralysis is generally not painful. Discomfort comes from compensatory muscle tension during effortful speech, dryness, and throat clearing. Persistent throat pain warrants evaluation for other causes including malignancy, infection, or laryngopharyngeal reflux.

faqs.question: Do children get vocal cord paralysis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Causes in children include birth trauma, Arnold-Chiari malformation, hydrocephalus, congenital cardiac surgery (especially patent ductus repair), and idiopathic. Many congenital cases resolve over 6-12 months. Management is by pediatric otolaryngologists and may include feeding modification or temporary tracheostomy.

faqs.question: What is bilateral vocal cord paralysis?
faqs.answer: Bilateral vocal cord paralysis means both folds are immobile. It frequently presents with stridor and airway obstruction because both folds stay near the midline. Common causes include bilateral thyroid surgery, brainstem stroke, and neurodegenerative disease. Often requires tracheostomy or airway-enlarging surgery.

faqs.question: How long should I wait before considering permanent surgery?
faqs.answer: Most laryngologists recommend at least 6-12 months of observation, voice therapy, and temporary injection medialization to allow spontaneous recovery, except when the cause clearly precludes recovery (e.g., transected nerve, tumor invasion). Laryngeal EMG can help predict recovery and refine timing.

faqs.question: Can vocal cord paralysis affect breathing?
faqs.answer: Unilateral paralysis usually does not cause significant airway obstruction. Bilateral paralysis often presents with inspiratory stridor and exercise intolerance because both folds remain near the midline. Acute or progressive stridor requires emergency assessment and possible airway intervention.

faqs.question: Is reflux related to vocal cord paralysis?
faqs.answer: Reflux does not cause vocal cord paralysis directly but frequently coexists and can produce or worsen secondary laryngeal inflammation, edema, and granulomas that impair recovery. Treatment of laryngopharyngeal reflux with dietary changes and proton-pump inhibitors is often part of comprehensive management.

faqs.question: When should I see an ENT for hoarseness?
faqs.answer: See an ENT for hoarseness lasting more than 2-3 weeks, hoarseness with associated weight loss or neck mass, hoarseness after recent surgery (thyroid, cervical spine, cardiothoracic), or hoarseness in a smoker over age 50. Early evaluation identifies treatable causes and avoids delayed diagnosis of malignancy.

faqs.question: Does insurance cover vocal cord paralysis treatment?
faqs.answer: Most US and international insurance plans cover diagnostic laryngoscopy, voice therapy, and medically necessary surgical procedures for vocal cord paralysis. Coverage details vary; office-based injections may have specific authorization requirements. Patients should confirm coverage and out-of-pocket costs with their insurer before scheduled procedures.
